denoland/deno#7123.body:
The re-exports of assertion methods from `std/testing` defined in `std/node/assert` are named incorrectly, because `assertEquals()` performs a [strict deep equality check](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/master/std/testing/asserts.ts#L154-L155), so it should be called `deepStrictEqual()` to match the expected behavior described in the corresponding Node.js [docs](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v14.x/docs/api/assert.html#assert_assert_deepstrictequal_actual_expected_message). The same applies to `assertNotEquals()` and `notDeepStrictEqual()`. 

In Node, `assert()` is just an [alias](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v14.x/docs/api/assert.html#assert_assert_ok_value_message) of `ok()`. This PR adds that alias as well, so it closes #7102.

Node's `equals()` and `notEquals()` methods are deprecated anyway, so I'm not sure we should implement them at all.

This PR also adds `assertNotStrictEquals()` in `std/testing` and re-exports it in `std/node/assert` as `notStrictEqual()`.
denoland/deno#7123.title: Fix std/node/assert misnamed exports, close #7102

denoland/deno#12067.body:
Deno's implementation of structured serialize currently supports serializing the JS built-in `SharedArrayBuffer`, and transferring the JS built-in `ArrayBuffer` as well as the `MessagePort` web API. With #11823 it will also support serializing the wasm built-in `WebAssembly.Module`.

But Deno also implements some web APIs that per the spec should be serializable or transferable but aren't in Deno's implementation. In particular, `DOMException`, `File`, `Blob` and `CryptoKey` should be serializable; and `ReadableStream`, `WritableStream` and `TransformStream` should be transferable.

Implementing this would probably need adding some `Deno.core` API to define serialization and transfer steps, possibly in connection to the existing `Deno.core.createHostObject()`. This would also allow refactoring the implementation of `MessagePort`.
denoland/deno#12067.title: Implement serializable and transferrable web platform APIs

denoland/deno#23268.body:
I'm building an application in Deno that uses [lmdb-js](https://github.com/kriszyp/lmdb-js), and we hit a snag where asyncronous transactions don't work on Deno, which we believe is due to this issue (from the lmdb-js README):

> Note that Deno and Bun's support for NAPI is not very stable yet, and currently asynchronous transactions (transaction method) are not supported (Bun issue https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/issues/158#issuecomment-1126624085).

From what we can tell, the "Asynchronous thread-safe function calls" family of functions is not implemented, and may be what's causing this:

![image](https://github.com/denoland/deno/assets/3639540/935393f7-a9a0-4cfb-8dcd-52e9e3e55526)

Having Deno + LMDB would be a big win for us. And I believe there's unexplored potential with Deno KV as well (eg creating a wrapper for lmdb-js that implements the Deno.KV interface https://github.com/kriszyp/lmdb-js/discussions/236)

cc @kriszyp @xyzshantaram
denoland/deno#23268.title: Support lmdb-js

denoland/deno#23562.body:
Version: Deno 1.42.4

The deno polyfill for `node fsPromises.watch()` does not appear to return an `AsyncIterator` [as documented](https://nodejs.org/docs/v20.12.1/api/fs.html#fspromiseswatchfilename-options) here for node.

With deno run, using the returned watcher with `for await` throws `TypeError: watcher is not async iterable`.
The same code works with node. 

```
$ deno run fswatch-node.js
✅ Granted read access to <CWD>.
Watching for file changes in /Users/jldec/opral/deno, press Ctrl+C to exit.
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: watcher is not async iterable
  for await (const event of watcher) {
                            ^
    at run (file:///Users/jldec/opral/deno/fswatch-node.js:8:29)
    at file:///Users/jldec/opral/deno/fswatch-node.js:13:1
    
$ node fswatch-node.js 
Watching for file changes in /Users/jldec/opral/deno-node-fs-promises-watch, press Ctrl+C to exit.
>>>> event { eventType: 'rename', filename: 'foo.bar' }
```

## repro

**[fswatch-node.js](https://github.com/jldec/deno-node-fs-promises-watch/blob/main/fswatch-node.js)** from: https://github.com/jldec/deno-node-fs-promises-watch
```js
import process from "node:process"
import fs from "node:fs/promises"

async function run() {
  const watcher = fs.watch(process.cwd(), { recursive: true })
  console.log(`Watching for file changes in ${process.cwd()}, press Ctrl+C to exit.`);

  for await (const event of watcher) {
    console.log(">>>> event", event)
  }
}

run()
```

Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/ext/node/polyfills/_fs/_fs_watch.ts#L126C5-L126C18
denoland/deno#23562.title: node:fs/promises watcher polyfill is not AsyncIterable

denoland/deno#24165.body:
## Description

The way `mkdir()` works in Deno seems to differ from Node.js, despite having the same documentation. Using subsequent methods produces unexpected behaviour.

## Steps to Reproduce

Set up a project like so:

```
├── template
│   ├── 1
│   │   └── 1.txt
│   └── 2
│       └── 2.txt
└── test
    ├── index.js
    └── package.json
```

index.js has this:

```TypeScript
import path from "node:path";
import { mkdir, cp } from "node:fs/promises";

const root = path.resolve("./output/here/");

const dir = await mkdir(root, { recursive: true });
console.log(dir);

await cp(new URL("../template/1", import.meta.url), root, { recursive: true });
```

package.json has this:

```JSON
{
	"type": "module"
}
```

Run `deno run -A test/index.js` and the output will be `undefined`, even though `recursive` was `true`.

Remove the resulting directory and run `node test/index.js`. The output is the first directory created as documented.

<hr>

In addition, Deno seems to create a folder containing the files, which is unexpected behaviour (output/here/1). Node.js does not do this (output/here). This is likely due to `mkdir()` being improperly implemented in Deno?

## Versions

- Deno 1.44.1
- Node.js v20.12.2
denoland/deno#24165.title: `mkdir()` incorrectly returns `undefined` and produces unexpected subsequent results

denoland/deno#25776.body:
It should just do a `kill -0`, which just does error checking for the PID and doesn't actually send a signal. From https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man2/kill.2.html:

>       If sig is 0, then no signal is sent, but existence and permission
>        checks are still performed; this can be used to check for the
>        existence of a process ID or process group ID that the caller is
>        permitted to signal.

This issue causes `nuxt dev` to error on exit:

```
 ERROR  [unhandledRejection] Unknown signal: 0                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                12:33:59 PM

  at toDenoSignal (ext:deno_node/internal/child_process.ts:387:11)
  at ChildProcess.kill (ext:deno_node/internal/child_process.ts:296:53)
  at kill (node_modules/nuxi/dist/chunks/dev.mjs:246:17)
  at Process.<anonymous> (node_modules/nuxi/dist/chunks/dev.mjs:291:7)
  at Object.onceWrapper (ext:deno_node/_events.mjs:518:26)
  at Process.emit (ext:deno_node/_events.mjs:405:35)
  at Process.emit (node:process:416:40)
  at Process.exit (node:process:61:13)
  at Process.<anonymous> (node_modules/nuxi/dist/chunks/index2.mjs:29886:42)
  at Object.onceWrapper (ext:deno_node/_events.mjs:516:28)
```
denoland/deno#25776.title: node:child_process.kill(0) throws

---

I looked into this. The issue is that the named export of `execPath` from `node:process` is not actually a string, but an object that tries to act like a string: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/55c22ee1bd8e5b108b8b13517150c3cfadf4d7f9/ext/node/polyfills/process.ts#L344-L357
denoland/deno#25779.title: `nuxt preview` falls in latest canary

denoland/deno#25929.body:
The latest Deno 2 release candidate crashes inside the Node compatibility function `os.cpus()` when run on an Android device under Termux. To reproduce:

```
$ curl -O https://dl.deno.land/release/v2.0.0-rc.7/deno-aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu.zip
$ unzip deno-aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu.zip
$ apt install glibc-repo
$ apt install glibc-runner
$ grun ./deno eval 'console.log("success")'
success
$ echo 'import {cpus} from "node:os";' >cpus.js
$ echo 'console.log(cpus());' >>cpus.js
$ grun ./deno --allow-sys=cpus cpus.js
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Failed to get cpu info
  at cpus (node:os:63:10)
  at file:///data/data/com.termux/files/home/cpus.js:2:13
```

I don't have another ARM device handy, but I'm curious whether this affects all aarch64 machines or just the Termux glibc wrapper script.

Version: Deno 2.0.0-rc.7

denoland/deno#26121.body:
I have a npm monorepo:

- package.json
- deno.lock
- apps/sveltekit-front
  - package.json
- apps/deno-server
  - package.json
- packages/shared
  - package.json

sveltekit-front only has devDependencies because it is built as a static website
deno-server only has "normal" dependencies, no devDependencies.
sveltekit-front has the shared package in devDependencies
deno-server has the shared package in dependencies

I use docker to deploy both apps.

To create the deno-server docker image:
I copy the root package.json, lockfile, apps/deno-server and packages/shared then I run `deno install` inside the root directory (I cannot just run `deno install` inside apps/deno-server because I am in a monorepo, I need the packages/shared symlink)

The problem is that `deno install` installs all dependencies, even devDependencies that are not needed for production.

Maybe I am missing something.
Thanks
denoland/deno#26121.title: deno install --prod

denoland/deno#26133.body:
I'm installing `pyright`, and it works really well, but it's supposed to install two bin entries and Deno installs only first.

https://github.com/microsoft/pyright/blob/main/packages/pyright/package.json

```json
    "bin": {
        "pyright": "index.js",
        "pyright-langserver": "langserver.index.js"
    }
```

Workaround is to create `bin/pyright-langserver` manually with

```sh
#!/bin/sh
exec deno run --allow-all --no-config --no-lock 'npm:pyright/langserver.index.js' "$@"
```

Can this be fixed?

Version: Deno 2.0.0
denoland/deno#26133.title: Installing a package with multiple bin entries

denoland/deno#26156.body:
Version: Deno v2.0.0 (tested as far back as v1.41.3)

Below is a simplified version of this test:
* https://github.com/denoland/node_test/blob/main/test/parallel/test-worker-resource-limits.js

```js
import worker_threads from 'node:worker_threads';
const worker = new worker_threads.Worker(new URL("./worker.mjs", import.meta.url), {
  resourceLimits: {
    maxYoungGenerationSizeMb: 4,
    maxOldGenerationSizeMb: 16,
    codeRangeSizeMb: 16,
    stackSizeMb: 1,
  },
});
```
And here's `./worker.mjs`:
```js
import { resourceLimits } from 'node:worker_threads';
console.log(resourceLimits);
```
In **Node.js** it logs this:
```
resourceLimits: {
  maxYoungGenerationSizeMb: 4,
  maxOldGenerationSizeMb: 16,
  codeRangeSizeMb: 16,
  stackSizeMb: 1
}
```
And a simple large string allocation test shows that the limit is respected - i.e. it crashes with `FATAL ERROR: Reached heap limit Allocation failed` if the limit is hit.

In **Deno** it logs the default values:
```
resourceLimits: {
  maxYoungGenerationSizeMb: 48,
  maxOldGenerationSizeMb: 2048,
  codeRangeSizeMb: 0,
  stackSizeMb: 4
}
```
And note that the `16` MB value is not respected. I.e. it seems that the `resourceLimits` log in the log shown above are "accurate" to the *actual* limits that the thread is held to, but obviously these are not the limits we asked for when creating the worker.

## Related Context:
* https://docs.deno.com/api/node/worker_threads/
* https://docs.deno.com/api/node/worker_threads/~/ResourceLimits
denoland/deno#26156.title: Deno ignores `ResourceLimits` for `node:worker_threads`

denoland/deno#26331.body: Currently we only support `SIGINT` and `SIGQUIT`. Node/libuv support other signals: https://github.com/libuv/libuv/blob/fbe2d85bd5a5c370a8cacea92b3bdfbd9f98a530/include/uv/win.h#L78-L96.
denoland/deno#26331.title: support additional signals (SIGHUP, SIGKILL, etc) on windows

denoland/deno#26671.body:
Version: Deno 2.0.4
macOS: 14.2.1

# Repro steps

1. `deno` in Alacritty
2. `process.stdout.on('resize', () => { console.log(`${process.stdout.columns}x${process.stdout.rows}`) })`
3. Resize Alacritty window

# Expected

Printed console output with the process columns and rows.

# Actual

Nothing happens.

# Additionally

If you run the same `process.stdout.on('resize', () => { console.log(`${process.stdout.columns}x${process.stdout.rows}`) })` in `node` (I'm using v20), then you'll see the expected results.

Also, I've tested in Warp and Terminal TTY apps as well and got the same bug.

denoland/deno#26784.body:
Version: `deno 2.1.1 (stable, release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)`

### Prerequisites
```sh
❯ helm version
version.BuildInfo{Version:"v3.16.2", GitCommit:"13654a52f7c70a143b1dd51416d633e1071faffb", GitTreeState:"clean", GoVersion:"go1.22.7"}
❯ node -v
v22.11.0
❯ bun -v
1.1.36
```

Deno configuration file

```jsonc
// deno.json
{
  "imports": {
    "cdk8s": "npm:cdk8s@2.69.18",
    "cdk8s-cli": "npm:cdk8s-cli@2.198.267",
    "constructs": "npm:constructs@10.4.2",
    "tsx": "npm:tsx@4.19.2"
  },
  "nodeModulesDir": "auto",
  "tasks": {
    "run1": {
      "command": "deno run --allow-all npm:cdk8s-cli synth --language typescript --output debug-deno/d1 --app 'deno run --allow-all a.ts'"
    },
    "run2": {
      "command": "deno run --allow-all npm:cdk8s-cli synth --language typescript --output debug-deno/d2 --app 'npx tsx a.ts'"
    },
    "run3": {
      "command": "deno run --allow-all npm:cdk8s-cli synth --language typescript --output debug-deno/d3 --app 'bun run a.ts'"
    }
  }
}
```

### Minimal reproducible code

```ts
// a.ts
import { App, Chart, Helm, YamlOutputType } from 'cdk8s';

const app = new App({
    yamlOutputType: YamlOutputType.FILE_PER_RESOURCE,
});
const c = new Chart(app, 'debug', {
    disableResourceNameHashes: true,
});

new Helm(c, 'chart', {
    repo: 'https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts',
    chart: 'kube-prometheus-stack',
    releaseName: 'monitoring',
    version: '65.8.1',  // Somehow version 66.2.1 seems fine?!
    namespace: 'monitoring',
});

app.synth();
```

Run the tasks

```sh
deno task run1
deno task run2
deno task run3
```

### Result

These commands should create `debug-deno/d1`, `debug-deno/d2` and `debug-deno/d3` directories respectively.

Compare these 3 files
```sh
debug-deno/d1/ConfigMap.monitoring-kube-prometheus-k8s-resources-multicluster.k8s.yaml # deno version is bugged
debug-deno/d2/ConfigMap.monitoring-kube-prometheus-k8s-resources-multicluster.k8s.yaml # npx version is correct
debug-deno/d3/ConfigMap.monitoring-kube-prometheus-k8s-resources-multicluster.k8s.yaml # bun version is correct
```

For your convenience I uploaded these 2 files here as well.

denoland/deno#26874.body:
The Deno doc [here](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/fundamentals/linting_and_formatting/#formatting) says,

> The formatter can be configured in a [deno.json](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/fundamentals/configuration/) file. You can specify custom rules, plugins, and settings to tailor the formatting process to your needs.

but never says how. But it says the underlying formatter is Dprint, so presumably the above can be accomplished with the Dprint options documented [here](https://dprint.dev/plugins/typescript/config/).

But if you add any of those to deno.json, `deno fmt` complains.

This needlessly precludes the use of `deno fmt` for anyone who doesn't like the options it hard-codes outside the tiny subset that's allowed. For example, I can't prevent the formatter from messing up my braces and flow-control statements. But Dprint offers the exact options I want:

```
"bracePosition": "nextLine",
"nextControlFlowPosition": "nextLine"
```
denoland/deno#26874.title: Pass Dprint options directly from deno.json during deno fmt

denoland/deno#26907.body:
Hello,

I am using `@grpc/grpc-js`  in version `1.12.2`.

I am reporting a bug related to this issue on the owner's repo: https://github.com/grpc/grpc-node/issues/2853

Bug does not occur when using Node, only with Deno.

The steps to reproduce would be to make one successful RPC call, then restart the server and try to make another call.

Any idea why this is happening ?

Thanks...

Version: Deno 2.0.6
denoland/deno#26907.title: @grpc/grpc-js cannot reconnect after server restart

denoland/deno#27167.body:
Version: Deno 2.1.2, Windows 10 x64

I wrote a program using Node.js, TypeScript and a lib called [microjob](https://github.com/wilk). the program code below:

```TS
export class EnvChecker {
  static async checkRuntimes() {

    // 首先通过进程命令行获取运行时类型（node/tsx/deno/bun...)
    const willCheck = process.env.EC_ON_LAUNCH || false
    if (willCheck) {
      try {
        // start the worker pool first
        await start({ maxWorkers: 1 })

        // this function will be executed in another thread
        const res = await job(() => {
          console.log(`run time is: ${process.argv[0]}`)
          // doing some other tasks, skip here
          return 'Env check done.'
        })
        console.log(res)
      } catch (err) {
        console.error(err)
      } finally {
        await stop() // shutdown worker pool
      }
    }
  }
}

EnvChecker.checkRuntimes()
```

denoland/deno#27211.body:
Version: Deno 2.1.2

In the provided script the X.509 structure's SPKI includes the very same SPKI. The SPKI itself can be passed to `createPublicKey` but cannot be obtained through X509Certificate.

This is unexpected.

The very fact that `process.getBuiltinModule` is used to **again** ship incomplete `node:crypto` implementations is to Deno user's own detriment since it is used by modules to dynamically target Node.js APIs for their performance and ease of use when a pure JS fallback would be used otherwise.

```js
const spki =
  '-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----\n' +
  'MCowBQYDK2VwAyEAlBZ9GShvbQEtyyaGs+0Nd4aurH7ERq6UOIvXGb5/tXA=\n' +
  '-----END PUBLIC KEY-----\n'

const x509 =
  '-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----\n' +
  'MIIBoTCCAVOgAwIBAgIUde5G4y+mtbb0eRISc7vnINRbSXkwBQYDK2VwMEUxCzAJ\n' +
  'BgNVBAYTAkNaMRMwEQYDVQQIDApTb21lLVN0YXRlMSEwHwYDVQQKDBhJbnRlcm5l\n' +
  'dCBXaWRnaXRzIFB0eSBMdGQwIBcNMjIxMDExMTIyMTUzWhgPMjEyMjA5MTcxMjIx\n' +
  'NTNaMEUxCzAJBgNVBAYTAkNaMRMwEQYDVQQIDApTb21lLVN0YXRlMSEwHwYDVQQK\n' +
  'DBhJbnRlcm5ldCBXaWRnaXRzIFB0eSBMdGQwKjAFBgMrZXADIQCUFn0ZKG9tAS3L\n' +
  'Joaz7Q13hq6sfsRGrpQ4i9cZvn+1cKNTMFEwHQYDVR0OBBYEFAATzoAtBcYTcOdY\n' +
  'jkcQqsWXipnSMB8GA1UdIwQYMBaAFAATzoAtBcYTcOdYjkcQqsWXipnSMA8GA1Ud\n' +
  'EwEB/wQFMAMBAf8wBQYDK2VwA0EApfw+9jSO0x0IorDfdr5ZVGRBVgrfrd9XhxqQ\n' +
  'Krphj6cA4Ls9aMYAHf5w+OW9D/t3a9p6mYm78AKIdBsPEtT1AQ==\n' +
  '-----END CERTIFICATE-----\n'

const crypto = globalThis.process.getBuiltinModule('node:crypto')

const publicKeyObject = crypto.createPublicKey(spki)
const x509Object = new crypto.X509Certificate(x509)

console.log('got publicKeyObject\n', publicKeyObject)
console.log('got x509Object\n', x509Object)

try {
  console.log('got x509Object.publicKey', x509Object.publicKey)

  // Not implemented in deno, but it never gets here anyway
  console.log(
    'x509Object.publicKey.equals(publicKeyObject)',
    x509Object.publicKey.equals(publicKeyObject),
  )
} catch (err) {
  console.log('x509Object.publicKey error', err)
}
```

denoland/deno#27281.body:
Deno changes the order of microtasks depending on whether it runs in a REPL or in a file. I don't think this should ever be the case. Look:

```sh
$ # Create repro file
$ echo -e 'Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log("then"))\nprocess.nextTick(() => console.log("nextTick"))' > /tmp/file.js

# Run with Node and Deno
$ node /tmp/file.js
nextTick
then
$ deno /tmp/file.js
then
nextTick

# Do the same thing in a REPL and with eval
$ deno
Deno 2.1.1
exit using ctrl+d, ctrl+c, or close()
REPL is running with all permissions allowed.
To specify permissions, run `deno repl` with allow flags.
> import '/tmp/file.js'
then
nextTick
[Module: null prototype] {  }
> function repro() {
    Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log("then"))
    process.nextTick(() => console.log("nextTick"))
}
undefined
> repro()
nextTick
then
undefined
> eval(Deno.readTextFileSync('/tmp/file.js'))
nextTick
then
undefined
> await eval(await Deno.readTextFile('/tmp/file.js'))
then
nextTick
undefined
> 
$
```

_Originally posted by @KnorpelSenf in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/22458#issuecomment-2500745522_
denoland/deno#27281.title: bug: `process.nextTick` changes the order of microtasks based on where it is executed

denoland/deno#27468.body:
Version: deno 2.1.4 windows 10 64bit
We created a simple database with npm's duckdb DB library.
In node.js, a single file named duckdb-direct.duckdb is created after program execution.
This is normal behavior.

However, when the same process is executed in Deno, after the program is completed
will leave a duckdb-direct.duckdb.wal file in addition to the duckdb-direct.duckdb file.

We have created a repository of the complete reproduced code below.
https://github.com/fushihara/duckdb-node-deno-issue

I am using only one library, the following duckdb library.
https://www.npmjs.com/package/duckdb

In node.js, however, only the duckdb-direct.duckdb file is created,
Deno creates two files: duckdb-direct.duckdb and duckdb-direct.duckdb.wal
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/78731f7a-38d8-4065-b29d-b766259551b7)
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1345eb45-4fc2-4cae-a3c3-2388354f8c47)

Since the behavior is different from node.js, we determined that it is a node compatibility issue with deno, so we create an issue in the deno repository.
denoland/deno#27468.title: Behavior is different from node with duckdb library

denoland/deno#27523.body:
Version: Deno 2.3.1
Related: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/24989#issuecomment-2567191851

Deno, with its `node:crypto` implementation, doesn't support doing ECDSA with secp256k1, only ECDH is supported (which is a different thing).

This code works on Node.js but throws on Deno:

```ts
import * as crypt from 'node:crypto';
crypt.generateKeyPair('ec', { namedCurve: 'secp256k1' }, console.log);
```

Used in [`@atcute/crypto`](https://github.com/mary-ext/atcute/tree/trunk/packages/utilities/crypto) to [validate most signatures in AT Protocol](https://atproto.com/specs/cryptography), as secp256k1 is more readily available in KMS/HSMs.

Web3/cryptocurrency use cases would also benefit from this addition.

Currently, `@atcute/crypto` mitigates this lack of support by adding an [imports condition for Deno](https://github.com/mary-ext/atcute/blob/7fc523522bdd13c6562b06cf47ad87a715414743/packages/utilities/crypto/package.json#L28-L35), it would be nice if Deno actually supported this to avoid using the JS-based [`@noble/secp256k1`](https://github.com/paulmillr/noble-secp256k1) library for signing and verification.
denoland/deno#27523.title: `node:crypto` implementation doesn't support ECDSA secp256k1

denoland/deno#28016.body:
Version: Deno 2.1.9

I encountered an issue with `setImmediate` in Deno, which does not seem to work as expected.

```js
import { setImmediate } from "node:timers";

let ticks = 0;
const startTime = performance.now();
const loop = () => {
  ticks++
  if (performance.now() - startTime > 1000) {
    console.log(ticks, "ticks per second");
    return;
  }
  setImmediate(loop);
}
loop();
```

The code above produces the following results on my machine:

```
deno 764 ticks per second
node 619805 ticks per second
bun  1093140 ticks per second
```

When replacing `setImmediate(loop)` with `setTimeout(loop, 0)` or `setTimeout(loop, 1)`, the results are:

```
deno 767 ticks per second
node 786 ticks per second
bun  997 ticks per second
```

It is extremely strange that the `setImmediate` function starts so few tasks per second

I will also add that I have tried many different methods:
`queueMicrotask` unfortunately blocks event loops
`setInterval` behaves similarly to `setTimeout`.

## Expectations & questions:

1. I would expect Deno not to have to wait ~1ms before executing the next micro/macro task.
2. Is there any way to achieve performance similar to Node.js in Deno?
3. How can I efficiently delegate asynchronous tasks without blocking the event loop and without such a long wait time?

## Context:

I'm building a game server that performs hundreds of small synchronous operations around 1000 updates per tick.
A naive synchronous loop that updates each entity blocks the event loop for user interactions.
In one second, I update the game world's physics 30 times, which means approximately 30,000 small updates per second.
When performed synchronously, the performance is sufficient. However, when trying to avoid blocking the event loop, 764 ticks per second is too low.
denoland/deno#28016.title: `setImmediate` function does not work as expected

denoland/deno#28120.body:
## Environment
- Deno Version: 2.1.10
- Related Projects: Next.js, Vercel edge-runtime
- Component: Node.js Compatibility Layer (Buffer Polyfill)
## Description
The current implementation of Deno's Buffer polyfill does not properly export the File class, despite this being documented in the official Deno API documentation. This omission causes runtime errors when using frameworks that depend on this export, particularly in edge runtime environments.
## Current Behavior
When attempting to access the File export from the Buffer polyfill, the following error is thrown:
Error: Attempt to export a nullable value for "File"
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:177:7)
## Expected Behavior
According to the [Deno documentation](https://docs.deno.com/api/node/buffer/~/File), the Buffer implementation should export a File class that extends Blob.
## Reproduction
This issue can be reproduced in projects using Next.js with edge runtime, where the edge-runtime attempts to access the File export from the Buffer polyfill.
## Technical Details
- Affected polyfill location: ext/node/polyfills/internal/buffer.mjs ([link](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/ext/node/polyfills/internal/buffer.mjs#L89)[)](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/ext/node/polyfills/internal/buffer.mjs#L89)))
- Edge runtime implementation reference: [edge-vm.ts](https://github.com/vercel/edge-runtime/blob/main/packages/vm/src/edge-vm.ts#L297)
## Impact
This issue affects:
- Next.js applications using edge runtime
- Potentially other frameworks that rely on the Buffer File export
- Any edge computing scenarios using Deno's Node.js compatibility layer
## Additional Context
The missing export creates compatibility issues between Deno's Node.js polyfills and existing Node.js-based frameworks, particularly in edge computing scenarios where the File class is expected to be available.
denoland/deno#28120.title: Buffer Polyfill Missing File Export

denoland/deno#28411.body:
Deno just copied documents from node, which has `chacha20-poly1305` in [createCipheriv](https://docs.deno.com/api/node/crypto/~/createCipheriv), but it only support these ciphers:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/ext/node/ops/crypto/cipher.rs#L22

Since RustCrypto already has [chacha20poly1305](https://github.com/RustCrypto/AEADs/tree/master/chacha20poly1305), it should be not too difficult to add it.

I'm happy to contribute to deno, I'll appreciate it if anyone can help me.
denoland/deno#28411.title: Support chacha20-poly1305 in node:crypto

denoland/deno#28445.body:
We should support users specifying the `http.route` and `error.type` attribute on automatically instrumented HTTP metrics.

To do this, I propose we copy the relevant attributes off the associated span on span completion. I suggest we do this as follows:

- The span for the HTTP server is created in Rust (as an `OtelSpan` struct) and is associated with the request.
- The span object can be exposed to JS with an op, similar to how `op_http_get_request_method_and_url` works. It takes the external for the `HttpRequest`, and returns a `OtelSpan`. The span is then put into `AsyncContext`.
- The user can now update the span (adding `http.route`, `error.type`, etc).
- When the response is finalized, we read the attributes relevant to the metric we'd like to record, in Rust, from the span.
denoland/deno#28445.title: [otel] Add support for specifying `http.route` on automatic HTTP server metrics

denoland/deno#30170.body:
Version: Deno 2.4.2 // Node v18.20.5

When doing `tls.connect({port: 443, host: '127.0.0.1', servername: 'example.local.', ca: ca})`, Node treats certificate valid names and the SNI servername domains equal regardless of they having or not a trailing dot. Deno does not. 

A full reproduction can be cloned [here](https://github.com/arnauorriols/reproduce-deno-trailing-dot-bug). The gist is, when fetching a server who's certificate has `example.local.` as a valid name, connecting to it using `example.local` works with Node but not with Deno:
```
$ node client.js 
TLS connection established
Certificate authorized: true
Received: HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: text/plain;charset=UTF-8
vary: Accept-Encoding
connection: close
content-length: 18
date: Tue, 22 Jul 2025 13:47:59 GMT

Hello HTTPS World!
Connection ended
```

```
deno run -A client.js
TLS connection established
Certificate authorized: true
Error: InvalidData: invalid peer certificate: certificate not valid for name "example.local"; certificate is only valid for DnsName("example.local.")
    at TlsConn.read (ext:deno_net/01_net.js:143:26)
    at TCP.#read (ext:deno_node/internal_binding/stream_wrap.ts:245:46)
    at TCP.readStart (ext:deno_node/internal_binding/stream_wrap.ts:91:17)
    at TCP.handle.afterConnectTls (node:_tls_wrap:150:20)
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:178:7) {
  name: "InvalidData"
```

Moreover, connecting with `example.local.` does not work with Deno either, with the error saying that the certificate is not valid for A, because the certificate is only valid for A:
```
deno run -A client.js
TLS connection established
Certificate authorized: true
Error: InvalidData: invalid peer certificate: certificate not valid for name "example.local."; certificate is only valid for DnsName("example.local.")
    at TlsConn.read (ext:deno_net/01_net.js:143:26)
    at TCP.#read (ext:deno_node/internal_binding/stream_wrap.ts:245:46)
    at TCP.readStart (ext:deno_node/internal_binding/stream_wrap.ts:91:17)
    at TCP.handle.afterConnectTls (node:_tls_wrap:150:20)
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:178:7) {
  name: "InvalidData"
```

Connecting with Node to `example.local.` works.
denoland/deno#30170.title: `tls.connect` does not normalize `servername` with trailing dot

denoland/deno#30243.body:
It seems to be an old bug that causes panic from v1.45.2 (may be found in earlier versions, but not tested) to the latest version v2.4.2.

Related to #13885

## How to reproduce
1. Make a pem and der
```
openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -pkeyopt rsa_keygen_bits:2048 
-out meow.pem

openssl pkcs8 -topk8 -nocrypt -in meow.pem -outform der -out 
meow_pkcs8.der
```

  "pkcs8",

  { name: "ECDSA", namedCurve: "P-256" },
  true,
  ["sign"],
);
```

3. Run the script through Deno.

denoland/deno#30402.body:
Version: Deno 2.4.4
OS: Windows 11 Pro

### Steps
1. Create a `src/index.ts` file and DO NOT create `.env.missing`
2. Run `deno compile --allow-net --allow-env --env-file=.env.missing --output=./dist/app src/index.ts`

### Expected
Successfully build a binary

### Actual
```
Task build deno compile --allow-net --allow-env --env-file=.env.missing --output=./dist/app src/index.ts
Warning The `--env-file` flag was used, but the environment file specified '.env.missing' was not found.
Compile file:///C:/Users/jon/projects/example/src/index.ts to ./dist/app.exe
Warning Environment variables from the file ".env.missing" were embedded in the generated executable file
error: Writing deno compile executable to temporary file './dist\app.exe.tmp-4f6be2f87253cac0'

Caused by:
    0: path not found
    1: path not found
```

### Workarounds
If I go ahead and create the `.env.missing` file, then `deno compile` works fine.

### Context
I was looking for a way to do `dotenv` style env file inheritance (based on development/production) and noticed the following worked fine with `deno run` but not `deno compile`:
```
// ✅ Success
deno run --allow-net --allow-env --env-file=.env --env-file=.env.development --env-file=.env.development.local --watch src/index.ts

// ❌ Failure
deno compile --allow-net --allow-env --env-file=.env --env-file=.env.production --env-file=.env.production.local --output=./dist/app src/index.ts
```

Usually I use `.env`, `.env.development` and `.env.production` as defaults that are committed into the repo, and then rely on `.env.*.local` files at dev-time or build-time, to layer in any further values. At run-time, local ENVs would still be expected to override/set any values. This all appears to work as expected except for the failure to build when an `--env-file` is not found.
denoland/deno#30402.title: Deno compile --env-file fails when specified file is not found

denoland/deno#30610.body:
Version: Deno 2.4.5

`deno install --global jsr:packageName` ignores the `deno.lock` file.

Ignoring the lock file is expected behavior when using `deno install` without `--global` (like `deno add`) to add dependencies to the current project, because the main project is the only `deno.lock` that gets used. All transitive dependencies are resolved by the version ranges defined in deno.json[c]. 

But when installing a **script or application** with `deno install --global`, I (and I would assume many developers?) expected that deno would use the deno.lock file stored in jsr to make for a repeatable installation.

However, what you get depends on your time of install, and the state of your deno cache, and may change over time. This is a nightmare for debugging issues across different installations.

* Every user who installs `jsr:packageName@1.2.3` likely has a different set of dependencies installed.
* If there's a regression in a newer version of a package, you can't reliably roll back to a package at a previous JSR version, because it may have experienced version drift.

# Possible Workarounds

1) Some manual workaround that requires downloading and specifying a lockfile w/ the install instructions.
2) Writing custom install scripts to automate the above. (But these install scripts would be subject to the same bug if they were published to JSR!)
3) Just requiring `git clone` + `deno task install` to handle proper installation + locking. (Not ideal. Relies on the git directory remaining in place.)
4) `deno compile` and ship binaries.
5) `deno bundle` and ship that. Or push it into JSR so people can `deno install` that. (I assume JSR maintainers do not want this to be a common pattern.)

None of these are great. If `deno install --global` just did the right/expected thing, everyone's lives would be simpler. Lock files are meant to lock down an application's dependencies. IMO we should be able to install an application from JSR w/ its lockfile.


# Extra Considerations

* Respecting the lockfile in JSR should probably also extend to `deno run jsr:package[@version]`. (Though I expect this would get implemented, since `deno install --global` installs a script that runs this.)
* This means *not* defaulting to the local deno.lock when running `deno run jsr:…` in a source code checkout. (Though maybe fall back if the JSR package doesn't publish a lockfile?)
denoland/deno#30610.title: `deno install --global jsr:packageName` ignores `deno.lock`

denoland/deno#30747.body:
## Summary
Deno v2.3.4 introduced a regression where the `enquirer` npm package loses the first character when typing responses to the second and subsequent prompts in a sequence.

## Root Cause  
Suspected cause: **"fix(ext/node): prevent stdin double read (#29353)"**

## Reproduction
```typescript
// enquirer_regression.ts
import Enquirer from "npm:enquirer@^2.4.1";

const { prompt } = Enquirer;

console.log("=== Deno v2.3.4 Enquirer Regression Test ===");

const first = await prompt({
  type: 'input',
  name: 'first',
  message: 'First prompt - Type "hello"'
});

const second = await prompt({
  type: 'input', 
  name: 'second',
  message: 'Second prompt - Type "world"'
});

console.log('Results:', first, second);
```

**Run:** `deno run --allow-read --allow-net enquirer_regression.ts`

## Expected Behavior
```
First prompt - Type "hello": hello
Second prompt - Type "world": world
Results: { first: 'hello' } { second: 'world' }
```

## Actual Behavior in v2.3.4
When typing the second prompt response:
- First character typed disappears
- User must type each key twice to see characters appear
- Final result missing first character

```
First prompt - Type "hello": hello
Second prompt - Type "world": orld
Results: { first: 'hello' } { second: 'orld' }
```

## Environment
- **Working:** Deno v2.3.3 and earlier  
- **Broken:** Deno v2.3.4
- **Package:** enquirer@2.4.1
- **Pattern:** First prompt works fine, 2nd+ prompts lose first character

## Additional Notes
- **Cannot create minimal reproduction:** Multiple attempts to reproduce this issue with dependency-free readline/keypress patterns were unsuccessful
- **Issue is library-specific:** The bug appears to be a subtle interaction between the v2.3.4 "prevent stdin double read" fix and enquirer's specific stdin/readline implementation patterns
- **Reliable reproduction:** While minimal reproductions failed, the enquirer case reliably demonstrates the regression across different environments
- **Potential wider impact:** Other prompt libraries using similar readline patterns may be affected

## Impact  
Breaks interactive CLI applications using enquirer, making them unusable for multi-prompt workflows.
denoland/deno#30747.title: [BUG] v2.3.4 regression: enquirer package loses first character on subsequent prompts

denoland/deno#30779.body:
## Problem

Currently, reading `/proc/pressure/*` files (memory, cpu, io) requires `--allow-all` permission instead of just `--allow-read`. This is overly restrictive since these files only expose system performance metrics and are safe to read.

## Current Behavior

```bash
# This fails with NotCapable error
deno run --allow-read script.js
// script.js: Deno.readTextFileSync("/proc/pressure/memory")
```

Error: `NotCapable: Requires all access to "/proc/pressure/memory", run again with the --allow-all flag`

## Expected Behavior

PSI (Pressure Stall Information) files should be readable with just `--allow-read` permission since they:
- Are read-only system monitoring files
- Only expose performance metrics (CPU/memory/IO pressure)
- Do not contain sensitive data like process memory or environment variables
- Are commonly used for system monitoring and performance analysis

## Affected Files

- `/proc/pressure/memory`
- `/proc/pressure/cpu`
- `/proc/pressure/io`
denoland/deno#30779.title: Allow /proc/pressure/* files with --allow-read permission

denoland/deno#30803.body:
All major package managers except Deno have a flag to install an exact version, but Deno doesn't have a way to do that. It's a hassle to remove `^` every time, so it would be nice to have a flag like `--save-exact`.

```sh
npm i enogu --save-exact
pnpm i enogu --save-exact
yarn add enogu --exact
bun add enogu --exact
```

Additionally, by specifying `save-exact=true` in your `.npmrc`, other package managers follow the behavior without the flag, so it's preferable for Deno to also respect the config.


Ref: [Should you Pin your JavaScript Dependencies?](https://docs.renovatebot.com/dependency-pinning/) - Renovate docs
denoland/deno#30803.title: Introduce `--save-exact` or `--exact` to install an exact version

denoland/deno#30912.body:
Currently, there is no way in Deno to perform async cleanup actions in watch mode. In Node.js it is possible to listen for `SIGTERM`:

```ts
import process from 'node:process'

process.addListener('SIGINT', () => {
  console.log('Received SIGINT, exiting...')
  process.exit(130)
})

process.addListener('SIGTERM', () => {
  console.log('Received SIGTERM, exiting...')
  setTimeout(() => {
    process.exit(143)
  }, 1000)
})

await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 10_000))
```

Which behaves like:

```
$ node --watch foo.ts
Restarting 'foo.ts'
Received SIGTERM, exiting...
Waiting for graceful termination...
Gracefully restarted 'foo.ts'
Restarting 'foo.ts'
Received SIGTERM, exiting...
Waiting for graceful termination...
Gracefully restarted 'foo.ts'
^CReceived SIGINT, exiting...
```

It is useful for closing DB connections (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/25609) and cleaning up things like watchers and FFIs (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12653, https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/20071#issuecomment-2135466035).

Using `addEventListener('unload', () => {})` doesn't work because, like browsers, it cannot prevent/delay unload (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12653#issuecomment-974862589, https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12653#issuecomment-975318720; https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/25609#issuecomment-2348320939 doesn't reliably work for similar reasons, `pglite.close()` is async API). Moreover, unload listener is not even called on restart when the main thread is blocked (like if you've a top-level await).

Proposed solution - send a signal (`SIGTERM`) while restarting and respect any signal listeners. This will be consistent with Node.js ([[1]](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/51df7b92bc0ab4f54f309d27f6ac49c7d6a5cbd1/lib/internal/main/watch_mode.js#L121-L138), [[2]](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/51df7b92bc0ab4f54f309d27f6ac49c7d6a5cbd1/lib/internal/main/watch_mode.js#L178-L189)) and, unrelated, but also what nodemon supports ([[3]](https://github.com/remy/nodemon?tab=readme-ov-file#gracefully-reloading-down-your-script), [[4]](https://github.com/remy/nodemon?tab=readme-ov-file#controlling-shutdown-of-your-script)). But it likely will need https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/7590 to be fixed first.
denoland/deno#30912.title: Support graceful shutdown in watch mode

denoland/deno#30964.body:
Version: Deno 2.5.4

On Node.js, the value of `process.argv` is a string array. On Deno, it is an array of getter functions.

## Actual Behaviour

```sh
$ # Create a repro file
$ echo 'console.log(process.argv)' > main.ts
$ 
$ # Compare Node and Deno output
$ deno main.ts 
[ [Getter], [Getter] ]
$ node main.ts 
[
  '/home/steffen/.local/share/fnm/node-versions/v24.10.0/installation/bin/node',
  '/tmp/repro/main.ts'
]
```

## Expected Behaviour

I would assume that both Node and Deno output a string array.

Note that it works well if you do `console.log(...process.argv)` as the getter functions are called implicitly with the spread operator.

denoland/deno#30986.body:
I've been unable to integrate Deno with a framework that auto-generates code into a local "vendor" directory (e.g., [sst](https://sst.dev/)), where its generated types are referenced via a triple slash directive.

In my specific use case, I'm trying to use [sst](https://sst.dev). After running `sst init` (per SST's [docs](https://sst.dev/docs/start/aws/deno/)), a directory named `.sst/` is created, which contains auto-generated code, including a `package.json` and dependencies installed to `.sst/platform/node_modules`. Finally, `sst init` creates a file named `sst.config.ts` in the root of the project, which contains a triple-slash directive to pull in SST's type definitions located in `.sst/platform/config.d.ts`.

```typescript
/* sst.config.ts */
/// <reference path="./.sst/platform/config.d.ts" />

export default $config({
  app(input) {
    // stuff goes here...
  },
});
```

Deno picks up on the types referenced via the triple-slash directive, but `deno check` follows the reference path and type-checks the code in `.sst/`. For example:
```bash
❯ deno check --config ./deno.json sst.config.ts
Check file:///path/to/deno-aws/sst.config.ts
TS2307 [ERROR]: Cannot find module 'file:///path/to/deno-aws/.sst/types.generated'.
    at file:///path/to/deno-aws/.sst/platform/config.d.ts:2:8

...

TS2307 [ERROR]: Cannot find module 'file:///path/to/deno-aws/.sst/platform/src/components/component'. Maybe add a '.ts' extension or run with --sloppy-imports
    at file:///path/to/deno-aws/.sst/platform/src/global.d.ts:336:42

Found x errors.

error: Type checking failed.
```

**Whether or not the type errors are legitimate is besides the point — `deno check` shouldn't type check these files to begin with.** Currently, in this scenario, there doesn't seem to be a mechanism to provide type hints through the LSP while simultaneously excluding the types when running `deno check`.

---

Configuration options in `deno.json` I've tried:

1. ` "exclude": [".sst"]`
3. `"compilerOptions": { "types": [".sst/platform/config.d.ts"] }`
4. using `links`/`patch`
5. The above in combination with `"compilerOptions": { "skipLibCheck": true }`

---

To create a minimal reproduction, you can run these commands:
```bash
deno init sst-deno
cd sst-deno
deno add npm:sst
deno sst init --yes
deno check sst.config.ts
```

I initially asked for help in Discord and was directed to file an issue ([discussion here](https://questions.deno.com/m/1415419846765580318)).

I tested using the latest version of Deno (2.5.4).
denoland/deno#30986.title: Allow `deno check` to ignore graph errors in dts files with `skipLibCheck`

denoland/deno#31005.body:
Include the process ID in the request payload sent to the permission broker:

```json
{
  "v": 1,
  "pid": 12345,
  "datetime": "2025-09-23T20:34:14.011647+00:00",
  "permission": "read",
  "value": "\"./README.md\""
}
```

**Use Case**

In enterprise environments, we often need to run multiple Deno processes concurrently with different permission requirements. Having the `pid` in the request would allow:

- A single broker instance to multiplex permission requests from multiple processes
- Better logging and auditing by correlating requests to specific processes
- More efficient resource utilization by avoiding the need to run separate broker instances per process

**Related**

- Original PR: #30826
denoland/deno#31005.title: Add process ID to permission broker request payload

denoland/deno#31080.body:
Version: Deno 2.4.3

At Framer, we use Deno for SSR-ing websites built by our users. We've recently moved to a new architecture where we run a single, persistent Deno process on a Lambda which spawns workers to import and render individual pages. After rendering is done, we close the worker.

The problem we're running into is we're seeing a good amount of "Too many open files" errors. We don't explicitly open any files, any FD usage is just `fetch`es and dynamic & remote `import`s.

We audited our code and believe we don't have any leaks, so I was wondering if it's maybe Deno e.g. holding onto the cached files forever and eventually running out of FDs? Especially since the errors seems to manifest itself during imports:

- Example 1
```
ssg error importing the main script TypeError: Failed reading cache entry for 'https://framerusercontent.com/sites/2TQK3lywRjgW70jVo45Nim/script_main.BR9eUujC.mjs'.
--
0: Too many open files (os error 24)
at async we (file:///var/task/ssg-sandbox-ondemand_worker.js:1:12074)
at async Promise.all (index 1) {
code: "ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND"
}
```

- Example 2
```
Download https://framerusercontent.com/sites/2RaQD1fuM3eJ6ex8mcFNte/eaJLTRmJ5-18.silXBMSa.mjs
--
TypeError: Failed caching 'https://framerusercontent.com/sites/2RaQD1fuM3eJ6ex8mcFNte/eaJLTRmJ5-18.silXBMSa.mjs'.
0: Too many open files (os error 24) (for '/tmp/deno/remote/https/framerusercontent.com/eeae12d80b1cb29e9103ed8eea7037056d96a30c71b395e7a0e0f30f083a4db1')
at async Promise.all (index 0) {
code: "ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND"
}
```

(Previously we used a persistent Node process that was spawning Deno processes for each render, and we didn't observe this problem back then.)
denoland/deno#31080.title: "Too many open files" in a long-running Deno process that does a lot of imports

denoland/deno#31135.body:
## Summary

The property `ppid` from `node:process` is not exported.

## Version

deno 2.5.4 (stable, release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)

v8 14.0.365.5-rusty

typescript 5.9.2

## OS

Linux Mint 22 Wilma

## Steps to reproduce

1. Create file `test.mjs` with the following content:
```js
import { ppid } from 'node:process';

console.log(`The parent process is pid ${ppid}`);
```
2. Run file `deno test.mjs`.

## Expected Behaviour
`The parent process is pid 4684`

## Actual Behaviour
```js
error: Uncaught SyntaxError: The requested module 'node:process' does not provide an export named 'ppid'
import { ppid } from 'node:process';
```
denoland/deno#31135.title: `node:process` does not export `ppid`

denoland/deno#31169.body:
Hi there,

I wanted to ask about the release schedule for Deno LTS versions. According to the [documentation](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/fundamentals/stability_and_releases/#long-term-support-(lts)), Deno 2.4 LTS was expected to be released on November 1st. However, it doesn’t appear to be available yet — running `deno upgrade lts` still installs version `2.2.15`.

Could you please clarify whether the release has been delayed or if there’s an updated schedule?
Also, would it be possible to update the public release calendar for upcoming LTS versions to reflect any changes?

Thank you in advance for your help!
denoland/deno#31169.title: LTS release schedule

denoland/deno#31176.body:
## Version

deno 2.5.6 (stable, release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)

v8 14.0.365.5-rusty

typescript 5.9.2

## OS

Linux Mint 22 Wilma

## Steps to reproduce

1. Create file `test.mjs` with the following content:
```js
import http from 'node:http';

const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {

  const earlyHintsLink = '</styles.css>; rel=preload; as=style';
  const earlyHintsCallback = () => console.log('early hints message sent');
  res.writeEarlyHints({
    'link': earlyHintsLink,
  }, earlyHintsCallback);

  res.writeHead(200);
  res.end();
});

server.listen(8000);
```
2. Run the file like `deno --allow-all test.mjs`.
3. Curl the page like `curl localhost:8000`.

## Expected Behaviour
In Node.js, the steps above give: 
- no output for curl
- `early hints message sent` printed on the server terminal

## Actual Behaviour
In Deno, the steps above give:
- `Internal Server Error` for curl
- and on the server terminal:
```
TypeError: res.writeEarlyHints is not a function
    at ServerImpl.<anonymous> (file:///my-files/test.mjs:7:7)
    at ServerImpl.emit (ext:deno_node/_events.mjs:436:20)
    at node:http:1627:18
    at new Promise (<anonymous>)
    at handler (node:http:1610:16)
    at mapped (ext:deno_http/00_serve.ts:360:24)
    at mapped (ext:deno_http/00_serve.ts:457:16)
    at ext:deno_http/00_serve.ts:744:29
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:179:7)
```
denoland/deno#31176.title: `node:http` does not implement `writeEarlyHints` method

denoland/deno#31179.body:
Not only is Deno install 2x slower than npm even before it hangs (taking 5 minutes compared to npm taking 2 minutes), but once it gets to the 854th dependency, it hangs for over 10 minutes before I stopped it. After that, running deno install would just do nothing and no loading indicator or anything would show.

The same happens with [One](https://onestack.dev/), another React Native framework that depends on Expo.

Version: Deno 2.5.6
denoland/deno#31179.title: `deno install` hangs for over 10 minutes when installing deps on new expo project

denoland/deno#31192.body:
Version: Deno 2.5.6

This code will fail:
```js
import vm from "node:vm";
const ctx = vm.createContext(vm.constants.DONT_CONTEXTIFY);
```

```
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: The "object" argument must be of type object. Received type symbol (Symbol(DONT_CONTEXTIFY)) 
    at __node_internal_ (ext:deno_node/internal/validators.mjs:124:11)
    at isContext (node:vm:193:3)
    at Object.createContext (node:vm:122:7)
```

According to the [Node.js docs](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/vm.html#vmcreatecontextcontextobject-options), this feature was added in v22.8.0 and v20.18.0.

So, this error may show that Deno does not fully support this Node.js feature yet.

However, this is not mentioned in the Deno documentation ([link](https://docs.deno.com/api/node/vm/~/constants.DONT_CONTEXTIFY)).

---
I opened this issue because I found that jsdom cannot run with deno.
```js
import { JSDOM } from "jsdom";
const jsdom = await JSDOM.fromURL("https://example.net/", {
  runScripts: "dangerously", // <-
});
```
denoland/deno#31192.title: `node:vm` does not not implement `createContext` correctly

pets requestBody: content: application/json: schema: $ref: '#/components/schemas/Pet' required: true responses: '201': description: Null response default: description: unexpected error content: application/json: schema: $ref: '#/components/schemas/Error' /pets/{petId}: get: summary: Info for a specific pet operationId: showPetById tags: - pets parameters: - name: petId in: path required: true description: The id of the pet to retrieve schema: type: string responses: '200': description: Expected response to a valid request content: application/json: schema: $ref: '#/components/schemas/Pet' default: description: unexpected error content: application/json: schema: $ref: '#/components/schemas/Error' components: schemas: Pet: type: object required: - id - name properties: id: type: integer format: int64 name: type: string tag: type: string Pets: type: array maxItems: 100 items: $ref: '#/components/schemas/Pet' Error: type: object required: - code - message properties: code: type: integer format: int32 message: type: string ```

## Expected(Node.js) ```bash % deno install ... % node --run lint No configurations were provided -- using built in recommended configuration by default. validating petstore.yaml... [1] petstore.yaml:12:5 at #/paths/~1pets/get Every operation should have security defined on it or on the root level. 10 | paths: 11 | /pets: 12 | get: 13 | summary: List all pets 14 | operationId: listPets Error was generated by the security-defined rule. [5] petstore.yaml:26:7 at #/paths/~1pets/get/responses Operation must have at least one `4XX` response. 24 | maximum: 100 25 | format: int32 26 | responses: 27 | '200': 28 | description: A paged array of pets Warning was generated by the operation-4xx-response rule. [6] petstore.yaml:55:7 at #/paths/~1pets/post/responses Operation must have at least one `4XX` response. 53 | $ref: '#/components/schemas/Pet' 54 | required: true 55 | responses: 56 | '201': 57 | description: Null response Warning was generated by the operation-4xx-response rule. [7] petstore.yaml:77:7 at #/paths/~1pets~1{petId}/get/responses Operation must have at least one `4XX` response. 75 | schema: 76 | type: string 77 | responses: 78 | '200': 79 | description: Expected response to a valid request Warning was generated by the operation-4xx-response rule. petstore.yaml: validated in 15ms ❌ Validation failed with 3 errors and 4 warnings. run `redocly lint --generate-ignore-file` to add all problems to the ignore file. ``` ## Actual(Deno) ```bash % deno install ... % deno task lint Task lint redocly lint petstore.yaml No configurations were provided -- using built in recommended configuration by default. validating petstore.yaml... denoland/deno#31413.title: `deno check --unstable-tsgo`

denoland/deno#31423.body:
TSGO crashes when linting a file that contains an npm-style css import. It looks like we're passing either no or the wrong module type. I can't reproduce this issue with tsgo itself, so I'm assuming it's something in our integration.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Run `deno init`
2. Run `deno install npm:@radix-ui/themes`
3. Create file `foo.ts`:
```ts
import "@radix-ui/themes/styles.css";
```
4. Run ` DENO_UNSTABLE_TSGO=1 deno check foo.ts`

Output:

```sh
panic: ScriptKind must be specified when parsing source files.

denoland/deno#31433.body:
Nodejs has recently added support for this  for faster object creation.

Would be great if Deno supported it as well.

See https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/59953
denoland/deno#31433.title: Support node_api_create_object_with_properties

denoland/deno#31434.body: These offer some performance optimizations for object key creation.
denoland/deno#31434.title: Support node_api_create_property_key_(latin1/utf8/utf16)

denoland/deno#31435.body:
Version: Deno 2.5.6

Hi,

I'm running npm:@redis/client@5.8.3 on a high throughput system. After ~2 days my Deno process crashes with

```
error: Uncaught RangeError: Map maximum size exceeded
    at Map.set (<anonymous>)
    at Timeout.<computed> (ext:deno_node/internal/timers.mjs:84:3)
    at Timeout.refresh (ext:deno_node/internal/timers.mjs:102:39)
    at Socket._unrefTimer (node:net:972:19)
    at Socket._writeGeneric (node:net:1085:8)
    at Socket._write (node:net:1100:8)
    at doWrite (node:_stream_writable:494:12)
    at clearBuffer (node:_stream_writable:651:7)
    at Socket.Writable.uncork (node:_stream_writable:429:7)
    at RedisSocket.write (file:///app/node_modules/.deno/@redis+client@5.8.3/node_modules/@redis/client/dist/lib/client/socket.js:230:22)
    at Class.#write (file:///app/node_modules/.deno/@redis+client@5.8.3/node_modules/@redis/client/dist/lib/client/index.js:603:22)
    at file:///app/node_modules/.deno/@redis+client@5.8.3/node_modules/@redis/client/dist/lib/client/index.js:610:24
    at ext:deno_web/02_timers.js:35:14
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:214:13)
```

I believe there could be a memory leak within the node:compat timers layer? If I isolate just Deno and Redis with a script that repeatedly sets and gets a key, I observe a continual increase in memory (RAM) usage 50mb to 2GB+ and an ever growing collection of Timeouts when I run a memory snapshot using Chrome DevTools.

I have a repo at https://github.com/rossholdway/deno-redis-timers-memory-test that shows the RAM increase and is where the heap snapshot is taken from.

Running the same script in Node (24.11.1) I do **not** see this issue, it maintains a stable memory / timeout.

Not sure how else to proceed with debugging this, so would appreciate if anyone from the Deno team could weigh in here.

Thank you
denoland/deno#31435.title: Possible timer memory leak in node compat

denoland/deno#31526.body:
The `wuchale` npm package uses `statfs` from `node:fs/promises` which currently doesn't exist in Deno. This makes the package fail.

See https://github.com/wuchalejs/wuchale/blob/138fadd9bbdf244fef69de275ba7ba81ef7a24c2/packages/wuchale/src/handler.ts#L6
Node Docs: https://nodejs.org/api/fs.html#fspromisesstatfspath-options

## Steps to reproduce

Run this code snippet:

```ts
import { statfs } from "node:fs/promises";

const res = await statfs(import.meta.filename!);
console.log(res);
```

Version: Deno 2.5.6
denoland/deno#31526.title: Missing `statfs` export of `node:fs/promises`

denoland/deno#31572.body:
I cannot run the newly installed `dx` command on my Windows machine, after installing alias with `deno x --install-alias`. I get an error when using `dx` in a terminal. ``` PS C:\Users> where.exe dx C:\Users\\.deno\bin\dx.cmd PS C:\Users> dx '.' is not recognized as an internal or external command, operable program or batch file. PS C:\Users> deno x No local commands found ``` ## Reproduction 1. On windows, run `deno x --install-alias` with Deno 2.6.0 2. Run `dx` ## Recommended fix I'm not very familiar with Windows `.cmd` syntax, but I discussed it with AI a bit, which gave a 2 alternative recommendations:
📝View AI 🤖 response 💡
So I believe it's best to change `./deno.exe` to one of the below: - `deno.exe` - will use PATH or relative deno command - `"%~dp0deno.exe"` - will use the relative deno command (more robust) I tried updating my `dx.cmd` locally with both options, and they both worked for me. ## Version deno 2.6.0 (stable, release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc) v8 14.2.231.17-rusty typescript 5.9.2 **Windows** Edition Windows 11 Enterprise OS build 22631.6199

denoland/deno#31583.body:
Version: Deno 2.6.0
OS: Windows11

If there is an npm package in `deno.lock` and the cache does not exist, `deno x` cannot be executed successfully.

## Reproduction

1. Create the following deno.json(c).
```jsonc
// deno.jsonc
{
  "imports": {
    "hono": "npm:hono@^4.10.8"  // Any npm package is possible
  }
}
```

2. Run `deno i` to generate `deno.lock`.
3. Run `deno clean` to remove the cache.
4. Run `dx` or `deno x` any cli. e.g.) `dx pindeps`
5. Display the following error:
```sh
error: Failed resolving binary export. 'C:\Users\xxxxx\AppData\Local\deno\npm\registry.npmjs.org\hono\4.10.8\package.json' did not exist
```
denoland/deno#31583.title: `deno x` cannot be run without npm cache

denoland/deno#31590.body: Version: Deno 2.6.0 In Deno 2.6, the LSP is not providing import alias suggestions based on the path mappings defined in deno.json, despite this being a documented feature. Instead, I'm getting relative path suggestions (e.g., ../../middlewares/doc). Expected Behavior: When typing imports, the LSP should suggest path aliases defined in deno.json → compilerOptions.paths. For example, with the configuration below, typing @/ should suggest files from the ./src/ directory. Actual Behavior: Only relative path suggestions (../ or ./) are shown, not the configured path aliases. Reproduction Steps: 1. Configure deno.json with path mappings: ```json { "compilerOptions": { "moduleResolution": "bundler", "baseUrl": ".", "paths": { "@/*": ["./src/*"] } } } ``` In a TypeScript file, try to import a module from src/ (e.g., import { something } from "@/..."). Trigger autocomplete (Ctrl+Space or equivalent). Observe that only relative paths are suggested, not @/ aliases. Environment: Deno Version: 2.6.0 OS: xUbuntu Editor/IDE: Visual Studio Code Deno Extension Version: Latest I've reported this issue multiple times on Twitter, Discord, and GitHub, but it remains unresolved. This feature is important for maintaining clean and manageable codebases with path aliases. Deno is a fantastic "Swiss army knife" tool, but this regression/issue is disappointing.
denoland/deno#31590.title: Deno 2.6 Path Mapping/Import Alias Suggestions Not Working in LSP

denoland/deno#31609.body:
Version: Deno 2.6.0

It seems `--preload` has no effect when using `deno bundle`, despite it [being listed as an option in the docs](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/reference/cli/bundle/#options-preload)

## Reproduction

foo.ts

```ts
console.log('foo')
```

bar.ts

```ts
console.log('bar')
```

Output with `deno run --preload foo.ts bar.ts`

```
foo
bar
```

Output with `deno bundle --preload foo.ts bar.ts` (file contents)

```ts
// bar.ts
console.log("bar");
```

Maybe I'm misunderstanding the purpose of `--preload` with bundle, but I was expecting it to be 

```ts
// foo.ts
console.log("foo");
// bar.ts
console.log("bar");
```
denoland/deno#31609.title: --preload seemingly does nothing with deno bundle

denoland/deno#31697.body:
Version: Deno 2.6.0, 2.6.1, 2.6.2, and 2.6.3

## Bug Report

`deno check` no longer resolves dependencies in a way that types can be inferred correctly for handler functions with Express and Koa. Deno incorrectly reports type errors now.

This did not happen with 2.5.6.

## Reproduction

```sh
$ cat test.ts 
import type Koa from "npm:@types/koa";
import type { Express } from "npm:@types/express";

declare const app: Koa;
app.use((ctx) => {
});

declare const app2: Express;
app2.post("/", (req, res) => {
});
$ deno check test.ts 
Check test.ts
TS7006 [ERROR]: Parameter 'ctx' implicitly has an 'any' type.
app.use((ctx) => {
         ~~~
    at file:///tmp/test.ts:5:10

TS2339 [ERROR]: Property 'post' does not exist on type 'Express'.
app2.post("/", (req, res) => {
     ~~~~
    at file:///tmp/test.ts:9:6

TS7006 [ERROR]: Parameter 'req' implicitly has an 'any' type.
app2.post("/", (req, res) => {
                ~~~
    at file:///tmp/test.ts:9:17

TS7006 [ERROR]: Parameter 'res' implicitly has an 'any' type.
app2.post("/", (req, res) => {
                     ~~~
    at file:///tmp/test.ts:9:22

Found 4 errors.

error: Type checking failed.
$ deno --version
deno 2.6.3 (stable, release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 14.2.231.17-rusty
typescript 5.9.2
$ deno upgrade --version 2.5.6
Current Deno version: v2.6.3
Downloading https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v2.5.6/deno-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.zip
Deno is upgrading to version 2.5.6

Upgraded successfully to Deno v2.5.6 (stable)

Release notes:

  https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/tag/v2.5.6

Blog post:

  https://deno.com/blog/v2.5

$ deno check test.ts 
Check file:///tmp/test.ts
$ echo $?
0
$ deno --version 
deno 2.5.6 (stable, release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 14.0.365.5-rusty
typescript 5.9.2
$ 
```

Note that the above commands demonstrate the following things:
- the error happens on 2.6.3
- the error does not happen on 2.5.6

In addition, I have verified that 2.5.6 is the last version that works correctly. All versions between 2.6.0 and 2.6.3 are affected and cause the exact same output.

denoland/deno#31719.body:
## Description

Deno's `node:sqlite` does not support numbered positional parameters (`?1`, `?2`), causing a "column index out of range" error. Node.js v24.7.0+ and standard SQLite support this syntax.

## Environment

- Deno: 2.6.3
- Platform: Linux (x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
- Node.js (for comparison): v24.10.0+

## Reproduction
```javascript
import { DatabaseSync } from 'node:sqlite';

const db = new DatabaseSync(':memory:');
db.exec(`
  CREATE TABLE users (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    name TEXT NOT NULL,
    email TEXT NOT NULL
  )
`);

// ❌ Fails in Deno with "column index out of range"
const stmt = db.prepare('INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?1, ?2)');
stmt.run('Alice', 'alice@example.com');
```

**Result in Deno 2.6.3:** Error: column index out of range  
**Result in Node.js v24.10.0:** ✓ Works correctly

## Workarounds
```javascript
// Option 1: Implicit positional (recommended for Deno)
db.prepare('INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?)');

// Option 2: Named parameters
db.prepare('INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (:name, :email)');
stmt.run({ name: 'Alice', email: 'alice@example.com' });
```

## Expected Behavior

Numbered positional parameters should work like in Node.js v24.7.0+ (fixed in nodejs/node#59350):
```javascript
stmt.run(value1, value2); // Should bind to ?1, ?2 respectively
```

### Important use case: Parameter reuse
```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?1 OR parent_id = ?1
```

This pattern is common in SQLite but currently requires workarounds in Deno.

## References

- [SQLite parameter syntax documentation](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_expr.html#varparam)
- Node.js #59340, #59350: Same issue and fix
- Deno #28134, #28154: Named parameters support
denoland/deno#31719.title: `node:sqlite`: Numbered positional parameters (?1, ?2) cause "column index out of range" error

denoland/deno#31765.body:
Version: Deno 2.6.3

I am working on a cryptography library for the Gleam ecosystem and in testing my test suite against the various Gleam targets (Erlang, Node, Bun, and Deno), I noticed that Deno's `node:crypto` `createHmac` uses the wrong block size for SHA-512/224 and SHA-512/256, producing incorrect HMAC values. Admittedly HMAC-SHA-512/224 and HMAC-SHA-512/256 are incredibly niche and I would highly doubt that any existing Deno code uses them.

## Reproduction

```typescript
import { createHmac } from "node:crypto";

const hmac = createHmac("sha512-224", "secret key");
hmac.update("too many secrets");
console.log(hmac.digest("hex"));
```

**Deno output:** `f3b72049f266b17a4cba8c8d9e167e53e5b3851ef417daa48b84d7ae`
**Node/Bun/OpenSSL:** `b6b4c9b0bce726416d1b41747acbf9ed886cc93aeebfa82a847a8113`

SHA-512/256 is also affected.

## Cause

In [`ext/node/polyfills/internal/crypto/hash.ts:234`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/7222e85d435b977de1ab810db067b86f29e6444f/ext/node/polyfills/internal/crypto/hash.ts#L234):

```typescript
const blockSize = (alg === "sha512" || alg === "sha384") ? 128 : 64;
```

SHA-512/224 and SHA-512/256 are truncated variants of SHA-512 and require 128-byte blocks, but `"sha512-224"` and `"sha512-256"` do not match the condition, so they get the 64-byte default.

## Fix

The easiest would be to add the variants or change the comparison to use `alg.startsWith("sha512")`. I am happy to submit a PR if it is helpful.
denoland/deno#31765.title: HMAC with SHA-512/224 and SHA-512/256 produces incorrect results using node:crypto polyfill

denoland/deno#31793.body:
### Description

Introduce a `deno create <package>` command as a first-class way to scaffold projects, similar to:

* `npm create <package>`
* `pnpm create <package>`
* `yarn create <package>`
* `bun create <package>`

Currently, Deno offers:

```bash
deno init --npm <package>
```

While functional, this is unintuitive and inconsistent with the ecosystem. The `--npm` flag is confusing (especially since there is no equivalent `--jsr` flag), and the mental model doesn’t match what developers expect from other package managers.

---

### Problem

* `deno init --npm <package>` reads as “initialize a project using npm”, rather than “run a project generator”.
* There is no symmetrical `--jsr` option, which makes the flag feel arbitrary.
* Developers coming from Node expect `deno create foo` to behave like `npm create foo`.

This creates unnecessary friction for onboarding and discoverability.

---

### Proposed solution

Add a top-level command:

```bash
deno create <package>
```

Behavior:

* Mirrors `npm create`, `pnpm create`, and `yarn create`.
* Resolves `<package>` from JSR first (if applicable), with a fallback to npm (or a clearly defined resolution order).
* Should allow `jsr:` and `npm:` prefixed for packages
* Internally can map to what `deno init --npm` does today, but exposed with a clearer, more conventional interface.

```bash
deno create jsr:@scope/template
deno create npm:create-vite
deno create vite
```

---

### Why this matters

* Improves developer experience and approachability.
* Aligns Deno CLI ergonomics with established package manager conventions.
* Removes confusion around the meaning of `--npm`.
* Allow `deno create` for JSR packages.

---

### Example

```bash
deno create npm:vite
deno create qwik
deno create @fresh/init
```

Instead of:

```bash
deno init --npm vite
```

### Related

- https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/28148
- https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/28220
denoland/deno#31793.title: `deno create`

denoland/deno#31861.body:
```bash
$ deno --version
deno 2.6.5 (stable, release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 14.2.231.17-rusty
typescript 5.9.2
```
I install a command using:
```bash
deno install --global --force --config deno.jsonc -A --name buildcmd src/cmd/buildcmd.cmd.ts
```
It creates 3 files in my ~/.deno/bin directory:
```text
buildcmd
buildcmd.cmd
.buildcmd.deno.json
```
The file named `.buildcmd.deno.json` is a copy of my current deno.jsonc file.

However, when I run `deno uninstall --global buildcmd` it fails to remove that file.

I've previously found that when I re-install a command, it fails to replace that file with the (possibly modified) current version of deno.jsonc. I was hoping to remedy that by always uninstalling the command before re-installing it, but it looks like that won't work either since the json file isn't removed.

Workaround is to manually remove that file it if exists whenever I uninstall a command.

denoland/deno#31871.body:
Deno 2.6.5
macOS 26.2

Repro:

```shell
git clone https://github.com/lgarron/comlink && cd comlink
git checkout deno-unref-repro
npm install

npm run build

# This should return, but it hangs.
npx -- deno run --allow-read -- ./tests/portability/refCount-false.mjs
```

Uncomment and edit the amount of `.unref()` calls in `tests/portability/refCount-false.mjs` to observe that 4 total calls (including the initial) are not enough, but 5 are.

This contradicts the documentation:

https://nodejs.org/api/worker_threads.html#workerunref

> Calling `unref()` on a worker allows the thread to exit if this is the only active handle in the event system. If the worker is already `unref()`ed calling `unref()` again has no effect.

This also reproduces using the default image for Codespaces (4 are not enough, but 5 are).
denoland/deno#31871.title: Need to call `.unref()` on a worker up to 5 times to actually unref it

Since Node.js's implementation of `DOMException` has been extended to accept an object that has `"name"` and `"cause"` properties as the second argument, Deno should be extended in the same way.

```js
new DOMException("an error message", { name: "FooError" });
```

related: https://github.com/tc39/proposal-error-limit-option/issues/1

denoland/deno#31931.body:
Deno 2.6.5:
```
Deno 2.6.5
> (await import('node:assert')).throws(() => {throw 1}, 'message')
undefined
> (await import('node:assert')).throws(() => {}, 'message')
Uncaught AssertionError: Missing expected exception: message
    at <anonymous>:1:52
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:187:7)
>
```

Deno 2.6.6:
```
Deno 2.6.6
> (await import('node:assert')).throws(() => {throw 1}, 'message')
Uncaught TypeError: The "error" argument must be of type function or an instance of Error, RegExp, or Object. Received type string ('message')
    at expectsError (node:assert:290:13)
    at Module.throws (node:assert:380:3)
    at <anonymous>:1:52
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:187:7)
> (await import('node:assert')).throws(() => {}, 'message')
Uncaught TypeError: The "error" argument must be of type function or an instance of Error, RegExp, or Object. Received type string ('message')
    at expectsError (node:assert:290:13)
    at Module.throws (node:assert:380:3)
    at <anonymous>:1:52
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:187:7)
> 
```

Node.js:
```
Welcome to Node.js v24.13.0.
> assert.throws(() => {throw 1}, 'message')
undefined
> assert.throws(() => {}, 'message')
Uncaught AssertionError [ERR_ASSERTION]: Missing expected exception: message
...
```

See https://nodejs.org/api/assert.html#assertthrowsfn-error-message, this is not the `error` argument, this is the `message` argument.

This is a breaking regression that has CI tests on Deno failing now after the update to 2.6.6 was deployed

denoland/deno#31936.body:
`FAILGLOB` behaviour seems like it would be nice, but it's actually problematic.

For example, the following commands current fail to run in deno task without turning off failglob (recently added behaviour):

```sh
> cat deno.json
{
  "tasks": {
    "example1": "rm -rf *.non-existent",
    "example2": "curl https://example.com?example=1"
  }
}
> deno task example1
Task example1 rm -rf *.non-existent
glob: no matches found 'V:\scratch/*.non-existent'
> deno task example2
Task example2 curl https://example.com?example=1
glob: no matches found 'V:\scratch/https://example.com?example=1'
```

`FAILGLOB` was initially turned on to help users not make mistakes, but it gets in the way too often. Turning this off wouldn't be a breaking change because it would just make more things work.

Ref: https://github.com/dsherret/dax/issues/352
denoland/deno#31936.title: Consider turning `FAILGLOB` off by default in `deno task`

denoland/deno#31957.body:
Backtrace:

denoland/deno#31983.body:
Version: Deno 2.6.x

How to reproduce the issue:
1. Unzip the attached file `test-bug.zip`
2. Open the `index.html` file via an HTTP server (e.g. run `npx http-server`) in Chrome or Firefox
3. Open the debugger console and notice that the following log is displayed: `Error code: 20`
4. Run `deno run ./script.js`
5. Notice that this log is displayed in the console: `Error code: undefined`

Expected result:
The same log `Error code: 20` must be displayed in the console when running the code in Deno.

This bug affects [zip.js](https://github.com/gildas-lormeau/zip.js) by causing it to fail in use cases related to action cancellation. As a consequence, this causes a regression in zip.js since the version 2.6 of Deno which supports transferable streams.

[test-bug.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24936327/test-bug.zip)

denoland/deno#32109.body:
We used `deno bundle` in its original implementation, then with its removal migrated to a custom build script with `esbuild`

Now that `deno bundle` is back we wanted to migrate back to using it, but one thing we are missing right now to recreate our current `esbuild` config is the [`keepNames`](https://esbuild.github.io/api/#keep-names) option.

I tried adding a `--keep-names` flag to `deno bundle` and this does what it should do, but before submitting a PR I would like to discuss the best way forward.

1. Would you appreciate a PR adding the `--keep-names` flag to `deno bundle` or is this a requirement too specific to be added as its own flag?
2. If you agree to have this added, should we just add the `--keep-names` flag to `deno bundle` and use `EsbuildFlagsBuilder.raw_flag()` internally? Or should we add `EsbuildFlagsBuilder.keep_names()` to https://github.com/denoland/esbuild_client/blob/main/src/flags.rs ?
3. Considering that there are more valid `esbuild` flags currently not supported by `deno bundle`, should we add a `--raw-esbuild-options` flag to `deno bundle` instead of adding individual `esbuild` flags everytime someone comes up with the requirement?
denoland/deno#32109.title: `deno bundle`: Add `--keep-names` flag
