denoland/deno#32239.body:
Version: Deno 2.6.9

This issues was found while trying to make all the emscripenten tests pass under deno: https://github.com/emscripten-core/emscripten/issues/26234

Running a test that crashes under node looks like this:

```
Error
    at run_js (/usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:7527)
    at wasm://wasm/f0d79f0e:wasm-function[4]:0x140
    at 1069 (/usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:7492)
    at runEmAsmFunction (/usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:6491)
    at _emscripten_asm_const_int (/usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:6554)
    at wasm://wasm/f0d79f0e:wasm-function[5]:0x150
    at callMain (/usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:8399)
    at doRun (/usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:8803)
    at run (/usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:8957)
    at removeRunDependency (/usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:5927)
```

But this from deno:

```
Error
    at run_js (file:///usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:7748)
    at <anonymous> (wasm://wasm/4b6c7e0a:1:321)
    at Object.1069 (file:///usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:7713)
    at runEmAsmFunction (file:///usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:6712)
    at _emscripten_asm_const_int (file:///usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:6775)
    at <anonymous> (wasm://wasm/4b6c7e0a:1:337)
    at callMain (file:///usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:8620)
    at doRun (file:///usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:9024)
    at run (file:///usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:9178)
    at removeRunDependency (file:///usr/local/google/home/sbc/dev/wasm/emscripten/out/test/a.out.js:1:6148)
```

note the `wasm://wasm/4b6c7e0a:1:321` vs `wasm://wasm/f0d79f0e:wasm-function[4]:0x140` in the second line.

In emscripten we rely on the stack strace format for things like ASAN and LSAN, and in particuler we depend on the `wasm-function[N]` part and we expect the offset to be in hex, which is specified in the Wasm spec: https://www.w3.org/TR/wasm-web-api-2/#conventions

Version: Deno 2.6.10 (stable, release, aarch64-apple-darwin)

An issue I found when doing a WebSocket server with Express in Deno.

```ts
import { createServer } from "node:http"; // remove node: to test with Node

const server = createServer((_req, res) => res.end("ok"));

server.on("upgrade", (_req, socket) => {
  const msg = "HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized\r\n" +
    "Connection: close\r\n" +
    "Content-Length: 0\r\n" +
    "\r\n";
  socket.end(msg);
});

server.listen(7777, "127.0.0.1", () => console.log("listening on :7777"));

```

Start it with `deno run -A issue1.ts` and try doing:
```
curl -sv --max-time 5 -H 'Connection: Upgrade' -H 'Upgrade: websocket' -H 'Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13' -H 'Sec-WebSocket-Key: QUFBQUFBQUFBQUFBQUFBQQ==' http://127.0.0.1:7777/
```

Or
```
npx wscat -c ws://127.0.0.1:7777/
```

Expected behavior with node - curl gets 401, wscat says "error: Unexpected server response: 401", server continues running.

Actual behavior with Deno - server crashes:
```
listening on :7777
error: Uncaught Error: write UNKNOWN
    at WriteWrap.onWriteComplete [as oncomplete] (ext:deno_node/internal/stream_base_commons.ts:90:16)
    at TCP.#write (ext:deno_node/internal_binding/stream_wrap.ts:315:13)
```

denoland/deno#32350.body:
Version: Deno 2.7.1

I was using deno 2.6.8 before. I have 2 env files, The .env file has my base environment variables used in development, then my .env.test has overrides I want used when tests are running. One of those variables that are in both files is DATABASE_URL. Before upgrading, the last one would take priority, after upgrading, the first would take priority.

The documentation for the --env-file flag still has a note saying that the last file inspected takes priority. I think this means either a bug was introduced in the order of precedence when a variable exists in multiple files, or the documentation needs to be updated to reflect the new expected behavior. I didn't see a change like this mentioned in the release notes, so I think this is a bug.

https://docs.deno.com/runtime/reference/env_variables/#.env-file
> When multiple declarations for the same environment variable exist within a single .env file, the first occurrence is applied. However, if the same variable is defined across multiple .env files (using multiple --env-file arguments), the value from the last file specified takes precedence. This means that the first occurrence found in the last .env file listed will be applied.

```
kyle@DESKTOP-V86D3M0:~/Projects/udibo$ deno --version
deno 2.6.8 (stable, release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 14.5.201.2-rusty
typescript 5.9.2

Found latest stable version v2.7.1

Downloading https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v2.7.1/deno-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.zip
Deno is upgrading to version 2.7.1

Upgraded successfully to Deno v2.7.1 (stable)

Release notes:

  https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/tag/v2.7.1

Blog post:

  https://deno.com/blog/v2.7


Please update to Deno v2.7.1 to avoid spurious new version notifications.

kyle@DESKTOP-V86D3M0:~/Projects/udibo$ deno eval --env-file=.env --env-file=.env.test "console.log(Deno.env.get('DATABASE_URL'))"

kyle@DESKTOP-V86D3M0:~/Projects/udibo$ deno eval --env-file=.env.test --env-file=.env "console.log(Deno.env.get('DATABASE_URL'))"
```

denoland/deno#32351.title: 2.7.1 build failure

denoland/deno#32357.body: Hey all! I put together a [kernel test bed](https://runtimed.github.io/kernel-testbed/) to evaluate how much jupyter kernels are properly obeying the jupyter protocol while also making sure that the runtimed crates (jupyter related rust crates) are working well with all the available kernels. Along its colleagues ark (R) and julia, [deno is at a 92% pass rate](https://runtimed.github.io/kernel-testbed/kernel/deno/), tying for second place! The only two the test suite were unable to exercise for deno were `interrupt_request` and `shutdown_reply`. Read the full report at https://runtimed.github.io/kernel-testbed/kernel/deno/

denoland/deno#32372.body:
Consider this code:

`index.cjs`
```js
throw new Error()
```

Running `deno index.cjs` resulted in:
```
error: Uncaught (in promise) Error
Warning Couldn't format source line: Column 228 is out of bounds (source may have changed at runtime)
    at file:///foo/index.cjs:1:228
    at Object.<anonymous> (file:///foo/index.cjs:3:3)
    at Module._compile (node:module:759:34)
    at Object.loadCjs [as .cjs] (node:module:788:10)
    at Module.load (node:module:677:32)
    at Module._load (node:module:535:12)
    at file:///foo/index.cjs:5:26
```

As you can see `file:///foo/index.cjs:1:228`, `file:///foo/index.cjs:3:3`, and `file:///foo/index.cjs:5:26` point to non-existent lines. The first two make sense when you consider that we wrap every commonjs file:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/8e5efb0509958ab5513b59c87cb83a98f1e897e6/ext/node/polyfills/01_require.js#L960-L972

denoland/deno#32622.body:
## Summary Running `deno install` on a project with storybook dependencies causes a panic in debug builds due to a duplicate key insertion in `DepEntryCache::store`. ## Reproduction ```bash mkdir storybook-test && cd storybook-test # Create a minimal React+Vite project cat > package.json << 'PKGJSON' {"name":"storybook-test","version":"1.0.0","dependencies":{"react":"^19","react-dom":"^19"},"devDependencies":{"@vitejs/plugin-react":"^4","vite":"^6"}} PKGJSON cat > vite.config.ts << 'VITE' import { defineConfig } from "vite"; import react from "@vitejs/plugin-react"; export default defineConfig({ plugins: [react()] }); VITE mkdir src && echo 'export function App() { return
Hello
; }' > src/App.tsx # Install initial deps deno install # Add storybook deno run -A npm:storybook@latest init --yes # This panics: deno install ``` ## Error ``` thread 'main' panicked at libs/npm/resolution/graph.rs:1057:5: assertion failed: !self.0.contains_key(&nv) ``` Full backtrace points to `DepEntryCache::store` being called with a package version that already exists in the cache. The call originates from `drain_pending_parallel` (line 2570) — it appears that parallel dependency fetches can resolve the same package version multiple times before the cache-hit check (line 2506) catches it. ## Impact - **Debug builds**: panics and aborts - **Release builds**: the `debug_assert!` is compiled out, so the duplicate silently overwrites — likely no user-visible issue, but indicates a logic bug in the parallel resolution path ## Environment - Platform: macOS aarch64 (also reported on Linux/Windows via #20610) - Deno version: built from `main` branch (reports as 2.7.4) ## Related Found while investigating https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/20610#issuecomment-4033491824 (storybook support tracking issue).
denoland/deno#32622.title: Panic in npm resolution: duplicate DepEntryCache insertion (debug_assert)

- [x] `test-child-process-spawn-typeerror.js` — fixed: use `validateInt32` for uid/gid
- [x] `test-child-process-spawn-timeout-kill-signal.js` — AbortSignal listener not registered when `signal` option is passed to `spawn` with `timeout` (`listenerCount(signal, 'abort')` returns 0)

- [x] `test-util-parse-env.js` — expects `Object.create(null)` prototype on result

- [x] `test-worker-cpu-usage.js` — `cpuUsage` argument validation differs

```
The Problem

The error is caused by the   (non-breaking space) entity. While web browsers and HTML parsers recognize   by default, strict XML parsers (which are used to render standard .svg files) do not. In XML, unless an entity is explicitly defined in the Document Type Definition (DTD), using it throws an "undefined entity" error.

Since Deno's new flamegraph generation is injecting   directly into the SVG output, the XML parser fails when you try to open the file standalone.
The Fix
```

denoland/deno#32873.body:
## Bug Description

There is a difference in how Node and Deno treat `{ terminal: true }` being passed to `require("readline").createInterface` in non-TTY environments. Node ignores the option entirely, and Deno throws an error.

An error is arguably the better API but since it does not match Node, some packages don't run well in CI on Deno.

## Reproduction

```sh
$ cat repro.js 
process.getBuiltinModule("readline").createInterface({
    input: process.stdin,
    output: process.stdout,
    terminal: true,
});
console.log("success!");
$ node repro.js < /dev/null 
success!
$ deno repro.js < /dev/null
error: Uncaught (in promise) BadResource: ENOTTY: Not a typewriter
process.getBuiltinModule("readline").createInterface({
                                     ^
    at Stdin.setRaw (ext:deno_io/12_io.js:177:5)
    at Readable.setRawMode (ext:deno_node/_process/streams.mjs:349:23)
    at Interface.[_setRawMode] [as _setRawMode] (ext:deno_node/internal/readline/interface.mjs:409:18)
    at InterfaceConstructor (ext:deno_node/internal/readline/interface.mjs:344:22)
    at new Interface (ext:deno_node/_readline.mjs:100:4)
    at Object.createInterface (ext:deno_node/_readline.mjs:203:10)
    at file:///tmp/repro.js:1:38
```

## Expected Solution

The option is ignored when not inside a TTY.

```javascript
const largeBuffer = Buffer.alloc(2 ** 32 + 5);   // 4GB + 5 bytes
largeBuffer.fill(111);

const result = Buffer.concat([largeBuffer]);

console.log('First 20 bytes of source:', Array.from(largeBuffer.subarray(0, 20)));
console.log('First 20 bytes of result:', Array.from(result.subarray(0, 20)));
console.log('Source length:', largeBuffer.length);
console.log('Result length:', result.length);
```

### Expected output
Source length and result length should be same as in the case of Node
```
root@KContainer:~/temp/55422# node repro.cjs
First 20 bytes of source: [
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111,
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111,
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111,
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111
]
First 20 bytes of result: [
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111,
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111,
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111,
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111
]
Source length: 4294967301
Result length: 4294967301
```

### Actual output
The initial 4GB is truncated and result only has final 5 bytes
```
root@KContainer:~/temp/55422# deno run -A repro.cjs
First 20 bytes of source: [
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111,
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111,
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111,
  111, 111, 111, 111, 111
]
First 20 bytes of result: [ 111, 111, 111, 111, 111 ]
Source length: 4294967301
Result length: 5
```
denoland/deno#32895.title: `Buffer.concat` truncates buffers larger than 4GB

Version: Deno 2.7.7

Hi,

I would like to report a bug in Deno. The PoC can be reproduced by the following steps:

### PoC

1. Create .env file

```
INNER_QUOTES="1: foo'bar"baz`qux"
INNER_QUOTES_WITH_NEWLINE="2: foo bar\ni am "on" newline, 'yo'"
```

2. Create envtest.cjs
```javascript
console.log(process.env.INNER_QUOTES);
console.log(process.env.INNER_QUOTES_WITH_NEWLINE);
```

3. Create envtest-dotenv.cjs
```javascript
require('dotenv').config();
console.log(process.env.INNER_QUOTES);
console.log(process.env.INNER_QUOTES_WITH_NEWLINE);
```
4. Run the code using commands:
```
 deno run -A envtest-dotenv.cjs
 deno run -A --env-file=.env envtest.cjs
```

### Expected output
```
root@KContainer:~/54134# deno run envtest-dotenv.cjs
1: foo'bar"baz`qux
2: foo bar
i am "on" newline, 'yo'
```
Both run commands should produce the same output

### Actual output
Output of dotenv:
```
root@KContainer:~/54134# deno run -A envtest-dotenv.cjs
1: foo'bar"baz`qux
2: foo bar
i am "on" newline, 'yo'
```
Output of deno with `--env-file` flag:
```
root@KContainer:~/54134# deno run -A --env-file=.env envtest.cjs
1: foo'bar
2: foo bar
i am
```
Both outputs are different. `--env-file` does not handle inner quotes or escaped newlines correctly.

### Reference
This issue was previously confirmed as a bug in Node: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/54134

denoland/deno#32950.body:
## Summary

`deno types` does not include any `Temporal` namespace, class, or interface declarations, even though Temporal is available at runtime (stabilized in Deno 2.7). This means the docs site at docs.deno.com cannot generate API reference pages for Temporal types.

## Current behavior

`deno types` only references Temporal as a type inside `Intl.DateTimeFormat.Formattable`:

```ts
export type Formattable =
  | Date
  | Temporal.Instant
  | Temporal.ZonedDateTime
  | Temporal.PlainDate
  | Temporal.PlainTime
  | Temporal.PlainDateTime
  | Temporal.PlainYearMonth
  | Temporal.PlainMonthDay;
```

There is no `declare namespace Temporal`, no class declarations, no interface declarations — nothing for the doc generator to work with.

## Expected behavior

`deno types` should emit the full Temporal namespace with all its types (`Temporal.Now`, `Temporal.PlainDateTime`, `Temporal.Instant`, `Temporal.ZonedDateTime`, etc.), similar to how WebGPU types are emitted (102 declarations).

## Impact

- https://docs.deno.com/api/web/temporal → 404
- https://docs.deno.com/api/web/~/Temporal.PlainDateTime → 404
- The Temporal category exists on the [Web APIs index page](https://docs.deno.com/api/web/) but all links are broken
- The docs build pipeline (`reference_gen/`) uses `deno types` output to generate API reference pages via `@deno/doc`, so missing type declarations = missing pages

## Reproduction

```sh
deno types | grep "namespace Temporal\|declare.*Temporal\|interface Temporal\|class Temporal"
# (no output)
```

Compare with WebGPU which works correctly:
```sh
deno types | grep "^declare.*GPU\|^interface GPU" | wc -l
# 102
```

## Environment

```
deno 2.7.7
```
denoland/deno#32950.title: deno types does not emit Temporal namespace declarations

denoland/deno#32966.body:
Version: Deno 2.7.7, macOS arm64

In Node.js many objects returned by `fs` (such as `FileHandle` and `Dir`) have `Symbol.dispose` and/or `Symbol.asyncDispose` methods so that they can be cleaned up by `using` or `await using` statements. Deno does not seem to implement these.

```js
// using.js
import fs from "node:fs/promises";

await using file = await fs.opendir(".");
```

Expected result: no output, successful execution

```
$ node using.js
$
```

Actual result:

```
$ deno run -A using.js
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Symbol(Symbol.dispose) is not a function
await using file = await fs.opendir(".");
                   ^
    at file:///using.js:4:20
```
denoland/deno#32966.title: node:fs objects are not disposable

denoland/deno#33010.body:
Version: Deno 2.7.8
Please take a look at this repo for a reproducible example:
[https://github.com/ufr-scf/deno-local-npm-package-issue](https://github.com/ufr-scf/deno-local-npm-package-issue)

Deno does not import a local npm package with no published version on npm correctly.

Setup is a basic monorepo with a node/npm typescript package and a deno package that uses the typescript package. Installation does not work when I use a package name that does not exist on npm. When I use the name of an existing package it does work.

From the [docs](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/fundamentals/configuration/#overriding-packages):

```
Overriding packages

The links field in deno.json allows you to override dependencies with local packages stored on disk. This is similar to npm link.

deno.json:
{
  "links": [
    "../some-package"
  ]
}

This capability addresses several common development challenges:

    Dependency bug fixes
    Private local libraries
    Compatibility issues

The package being referenced doesn't need to be published at all. It just needs to have the proper package name and metadata in deno.json or package.json, so that Deno knows what package it's dealing with. This provides greater flexibility and modularity, maintaining clean separation between your main code and external packages.
```

To me, this reads like I can just have a local package, not publish it and deno will install it just fine. But that is not the case.

This kind of setup (monorepo, shared code between deno + node/npm) seems pretty common/logical to me, so I would have expected this to work. 
Am I missing something here?

jsr-io/jsr#1152.body:
Within the last three days, running `npx jsr@latest publish --dry-run` has started failing on our repo:
https://github.com/eslint/rewrite

## Steps to reproduce

1. `git clone` the repo
2. `npm install`
3. `cd packages/config-array`
4. `npm run test:jsr`

## Output

```shell
$ npm run test:jsr

> @eslint/config-array@0.21.0 test:jsr     
> npx jsr@latest publish --dry-run

Checking for slow types in the public API...
error[excluded-module]: module in package's module graph was excluded from publishing 
 --> C:\Users\nzaka\projects\eslint\rewrite\packages\config-array\src\base-schema.js  
  = hint: remove the module from 'exclude' and/or 'publish.exclude' in the config file or use 'publish.exclude' with a negative glob to unexclude from gitignore

  info: excluded modules referenced via a package export will error at runtime due to not existing in the package
  docs: https://jsr.io/go/excluded-module  

error[excluded-module]: module in package's module graph was excluded from publishing 
 --> C:\Users\nzaka\projects\eslint\rewrite\packages\config-array\src\config-array.js 
  = hint: remove the module from 'exclude' and/or 'publish.exclude' in the config file or use 'publish.exclude' with a negative glob to unexclude from gitignore

  info: excluded modules referenced via a package export will error at runtime due to not existing in the package
  docs: https://jsr.io/go/excluded-module  

error[excluded-module]: module in package's module graph was excluded from publishing 
 --> C:\Users\nzaka\projects\eslint\rewrite\packages\config-array\src\files-and-ignores-schema.js
  = hint: remove the module from 'exclude' and/or 'publish.exclude' in the config file or use 'publish.exclude' with a negative glob to unexclude from gitignore

  info: excluded modules referenced via a package export will error at runtime due to not existing in the package
  docs: https://jsr.io/go/excluded-module  

error[excluded-module]: module in package's module graph was excluded from publishing 
 --> C:\Users\nzaka\projects\eslint\rewrite\packages\config-array\src\index.js        
  = hint: remove the module from 'exclude' and/or 'publish.exclude' in the config file or use 'publish.exclude' with a negative glob to unexclude from gitignore

  info: excluded modules referenced via a package export will error at runtime due to not existing in the package
  docs: https://jsr.io/go/excluded-module  

error[excluded-module]: module in package's module graph was excluded from publishing 
 --> C:\Users\nzaka\projects\eslint\rewrite\packages\config-array\src\types.ts        
  = hint: remove the module from 'exclude' and/or 'publish.exclude' in the config file or use 'publish.exclude' with a negative glob to unexclude from gitignore

  info: excluded modules referenced via a package export will error at runtime due to not existing in the package
  docs: https://jsr.io/go/excluded-module  

error: Found 5 problems
```

## Discussion

This is very confusing because the files referenced in the output are explicitly *not* included in the [`jsr.json`](https://github.com/eslint/rewrite/blob/main/packages/config-array/jsr.json) file. Further, these files are *not* ignored via [`.gitignore`](https://github.com/eslint/rewrite/blob/main/.gitignore).

~~I can't figure out how `jsr publish` even finds `src/index.js`. It's not even referenced in [`package.json`](https://github.com/eslint/rewrite/blob/main/packages/config-array/package.json).~~

It seems to be that JSR has suddenly started reading [`tsconfig.json`](https://github.com/eslint/rewrite/blob/main/packages/config-array/jsr.json) and assuming that `files` should be part of the published package? That's not a safe assumption to make in a world of bundlers. We have `tsconfig.json` set up primarily for editors before we bundle into a `dist` folder.

Also, how did this change happen outside of a new npm package version?
jsr-io/jsr#1152.title: jsr publish --dry-run suddenly failing (reading tsconfig.json???)
